question: MMLU and other knowledge benchmarks: what they measure and limitations.

options: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding): 57 subjects, 16k multiple-choice questions; tests undergraduate-level knowledge across STEM, humanities, professional. Limitations: data contamination (test items in training corpora), measures recall not reasoning, saturation by frontier models (~90\%+); successors: MMLU-Pro, GPQA (graduate-level), HLE (Humanity's Last Exam)

MMLU has only 5 questions (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (not supported by published research or production deployments) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)

Tests only mathematics (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)

Cannot be answered by any LLM (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
passive.explanation: MMLU (Hendrycks 2020): pre-existing exam-style questions spanning many domains. Few-shot or zero-shot evaluation. Initially highlighted scaling gains: GPT-3 ~43\%, GPT-4 ~86\%, Claude 3 Opus ~87\%, GPT-4o ~89\%, Llama 3 405B ~88\%. Concerns: (1) Contamination — many questions appear online, leak into training data; (2) saturation — top models near ceiling, weak signal; (3) MCQ format favours pattern matching; (4) limited reasoning. Successors: MMLU-Pro (Wang 2024) — harder, 10-option, less contamination; GPQA Diamond (Rein 2023) — PhD-level science, $<$40\% even for experts with Google; HLE (Humanity's Last Exam, 2025) — frontier-PhD-grade. Beyond academic: live benchmarks (Chatbot Arena, LiveBench), uncontaminated by design.

active.prompt: Match each LLM benchmark with what it primarily measures:

pairs.definition: Code generation; functional correctness via test cases; standard since Codex (2021)
Mathematical reasoning; grade school to competition; chain-of-thought essential
Crowdsourced pairwise preferences; Elo ranking; live, less gameable than static benchmarks
Real GitHub issue resolution; agentic coding evaluation; ~50\% best models on Verified set (2024)

active.explanation: Modern benchmark ecosystem: capability tests (MMLU, GPQA), reasoning (MATH, GPQA, AIME), code (HumanEval+, SWE-Bench, RepoBench), agentic (WebArena, OSWorld), multimodal (MMMU, MathVista), long-context (RULER, LongBench), tool use (BFCL, ToolBench), safety (BBQ, ToxiGen, HarmBench). Live: Arena, LiveBench refresh; uncontaminated.

question: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting: when does it help and why?

options: Add 'Let's think step by step' or few-shot reasoning examples: model generates intermediate steps before answer. Dramatic gains on math (GSM8K, MATH), logical reasoning, multi-step problems. Helps at scale (>~60B params); smaller models may degrade. Self-consistency: sample multiple CoTs, majority-vote answer — further accuracy boost
CoT hurts performance always (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)
Only helps with single-step problems (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations)

Eliminated by RLHF entirely (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (not supported by published research or production deployments)
passive.explanation: CoT (Wei et al.\ 2022): explicit reasoning before final answer. Forms: (1) Few-shot CoT — exemplars include reasoning chains; (2) Zero-shot CoT (Kojima 2022) — 'Let's think step by step' alone. Mechanism: enables sequential computation; each step is bounded compute, multi-step problems require multiple forward-passes worth of computation. Theoretical (Feng et al.\ 2023): CoT provably extends Transformer's expressivity. Emergent: scale-dependent; small models don't benefit (sometimes hurt). Self-consistency (Wang 2022): sample $N$ CoT solutions at $T > 0$, majority vote final answer; consistent gains. Process supervision (Lightman 2023, OpenAI): reward reasoning steps not just outcome → better reliability. Reasoning models (OpenAI o1 Sep 2024, DeepSeek R1, Claude Sonnet thinking, Gemini 2.0): trained via RL on CoT; much stronger math/code/reasoning.

question: Hallucination in LLMs: types, causes, mitigation.

options: Model produces confident incorrect information. Types: factual (made-up data), reasoning (logical errors), contextual (misreads input). Causes: training objective rewards plausible text not correctness; capability gaps; sycophancy. Mitigation: retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), citations, fine-tuning on factuality, reasoning + verification (process supervision), uncertainty quantification, tool use

Modern LLMs never hallucinate (not supported by published research or production deployments) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)

Hallucinations are useful features, not bugs (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))

Only happens with small models (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
passive.explanation: Hallucination: confident but wrong. Multiple causes: (1) NLL training favours common patterns regardless of truth; (2) lack of knowledge about niche/recent topics; (3) ambiguous queries; (4) RLHF can encourage confident-sounding outputs (reward gaming). Categories: intrinsic (fabrications), extrinsic (contradicts source). Mitigation strategies: (1) RAG (Lewis 2020): augment with retrieved documents; model conditions on factual sources. (2) Citation training: model produces citations alongside claims, verifiable. (3) Tool use: calculator for arithmetic, code interpreter for execution, web search for current info. (4) Self-consistency / verification: generate multiple answers, check consistency; chain-of-verification. (5) Calibration: train to express uncertainty (rather than always confident). (6) Process reward models. Still active challenge: even frontier models hallucinate citations, dates, statistics.

question: Agentic capabilities: how are LLMs being deployed as agents?

options: Agentic LLM: model takes actions via tools (web search, code exec, file I/O, API calls), in loop with environment, pursuing multi-step goals. Frameworks: ReAct (reasoning + acting), AutoGPT, LangChain, OpenAI Assistants/Computer Use, Claude Computer Use, Anthropic MCP. Benchmarks: SWE-Bench (software issues), WebArena (web), GAIA (general assistant), tau-bench (customer service)

LLMs cannot use tools or take actions (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)

Agents are pure marketing; no real capabilities (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (not supported by published research or production deployments) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)

Agents work only on demo problems (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)
passive.explanation: Agent = LLM + tools + loop + memory. Tool use: function/method calls structured via JSON or special tokens. Loop: model decides action, executes, observes result, decides next. ReAct (Yao 2022): explicit interleaved Thought/Action/Observation reasoning. Frameworks: LangChain, LangGraph, AutoGPT (2023 viral demo, limited robustness), CrewAI, Microsoft AutoGen. Anthropic MCP (Model Context Protocol): standardised tool/data integration. OpenAI 'Computer Use' (Oct 2024, Claude); Operator (2025) — autonomous browsing. Benchmarks: SWE-Bench Verified (50\%+ for frontier, e.g.\ Claude 3.5 Sonnet); WebArena (~20-30\%); GAIA (~50\% best); $\tau$-bench (mixed). Apps emerging: coding agents (Devin, Codeium, Cursor), customer support, research assistance. Challenges: error compounding, long-horizon planning, safety (broader action space).

options: Indexing: embed documents in vector DB; at query time, embed query, retrieve top-$k$ semantic matches, prepend to LLM context. Architecture: embedding model (e.g.\ Cohere, OpenAI text-embedding-3) + vector store (FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) + generator LLM. Challenges: retrieval quality, chunking strategy, context overload, conflicting sources, multi-hop reasoning. Variants: GraphRAG, HyDE, re-ranking

RAG replaces all LLM capabilities (not supported by published research or production deployments) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses)

Cannot use external documents (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic)
Vector embeddings are random (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
passive.explanation: Original RAG (Lewis et al.\ 2020): dense passage retrieval + sequence-to-sequence generation. Modern pipeline: (1) Document chunking — fixed size, semantic, hierarchical; (2) Embedding — bi-encoder (e.g.\ E5, BGE, OpenAI ada-3, Cohere embed-v3) → vector store; (3) Query embedding + ANN search (HNSW, IVF); (4) Optional re-ranker (cross-encoder for precision); (5) Generation with retrieved context. Variants: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) — LLM generates ideal doc, embed that; multi-query (query expansion); GraphRAG (Microsoft 2024) — knowledge graph + community summaries for global queries; ColBERT (late interaction); RAG with citations. Challenges: (i) retrieval still bottleneck — recent benchmarks show even SOTA retrievers fail; (ii) long-context LLMs (1M+ tokens) reduce RAG need but increase compute; (iii) attribution/grounding. Major use: enterprise search, customer support, knowledge bases.

question: Frontier capabilities and 'reasoning' models (o1, R1, Claude thinking): what changed?

options: Trained via RL on chain-of-thought to maximise verifiable rewards (math/code unit tests, etc.). Models 'think' for thousands of tokens before responding; substantial gains on hard math (IMO problems, AIME, FrontierMath), competitive coding (Codeforces, IOI). Tradeoff: test-time compute $\sim 100\times$ for hardest queries. Frontier 2024-25: OpenAI o1/o3, DeepSeek R1 (open), Claude 3.7 Sonnet thinking, Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking

No qualitative change; same as 2022 LLMs (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (not supported by published research or production deployments)

Reasoning models are tiny BERT variants (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)

Test-time compute irrelevant; only training matters (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
passive.explanation: OpenAI o1 (Sep 2024): first widely-deployed 'reasoning' model. Mechanism: RL on CoT data, learn to allocate test-time compute for hard problems. Scales 'inference compute' as new dimension beyond train-compute and data. Performance: o1-preview reached ~89th percentile on Codeforces; o1 ~$83\%$ on AIME 2024 vs.\ $13\%$ for GPT-4o; FrontierMath ~$25\%$. DeepSeek R1 (Jan 2025): open-source matching o1 performance; published methodology (R1-Zero pure RL, then R1 with mixed training); distilled to smaller models. Claude 3.7 Sonnet (Feb 2025): hybrid reasoning, user-controllable budget. Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking (Dec 2024). New scaling regime: test-time compute scaling laws (Snell 2024, OpenAI o1 blog) — more inference compute → better on hardest problems. Implications: economic/safety reassessment, capability evals must include reasoning budgets, hybrid use cases (chat vs.\ deliberate problem solving). Future: test-time search, learned verifiers, multi-agent reasoning.
